1576.description: The silver salmon of the Pacific run, pink-fleshed and prized.

1577.name: Blush Halo
1577.description: A blush with a saintly ring to it.

1578.name: Rosewater
1578.description: The distilled essence of rose petals — Turkish delight's perfume, in palest pink.

1579.name: Pink Salt
1579.description: Himalayan rock salt — pink from iron, mined from a mountain range, marketed as wellness.

1580.name: Ember
1580.description: The coal still glowing after the flame moves on.

1582.name: Lava
1582.description: Molten rock in its underground red — magma once it surfaces it's all press.

1583.name: Burnt Clay
1583.description: Clay fired past its first colour.

1584.name: Rusty Copper
1584.description: Copper streaked with its own oxide.

1586.name: Fire
1586.description: The element itself — combustion's orange-red signature.

1587.name: Autumn
1587.description: The season that shows off by dying — orange-red at leaf level.

1588.name: Terracotta Dust
1588.description: Terracotta ground fine — pot dust on the potter's apron.

1589.name: Pumpkin
1589.description: October's gourd — porch decoration, pie filling, coach conversion.

1590.name: Carrot
1590.description: The root vegetable that sees in the dark, allegedly — wartime propaganda's orange.

1591.name: Neon Carrot
1591.description: The Crayola carrot with a neon tube in it.

1592.name: Outrageous Orange
1592.description: Crayola's loudest orange — outrageous by name since 1972.

1593.name: Tiger Lily
1593.description: The spotted orange lily that grows wild along fences — tiger by pattern, lily by grace.

1594.name: Slippery Salmon
1594.description: A salmon that got away — orange-pink and gone.

1595.name: Salmon Nigiri
1595.description: The salmon slice over pressed rice — omega-orange, wasabi under.

1596.name: Pulp Friction
1596.description: Quentin's citrus caper — pulp, friction, and a briefcase of orange.

1597.name: Sunstone
1597.description: The feldspar with copper glitter inside — Viking navigation legend, Oregon's state gem.

1598.name: Dark Roast
1598.description: The bean roasted past argument — oily, dark, uncompromising.

1599.name: Bitter Chocolate
1599.description: Chocolate without the diplomacy of sugar.

1600.name: Otter
1600.description: The river's playful mustelid — wet brown fur, backstroke included.

1602.name: Tobacco
1602.description: The cured leaf in its auction-house brown.

1603.name: Pecan
1603.description: The pie nut of the American South — butter-brown shells.

1604.name: Ginger
1604.description: The root that bites back — warm brown with heat filed under fresh.

1606.name: Spiced Cider
1606.description: Mulled apples steaming in an autumn car park.

1607.name: Raw Sienna
1607.description: The Tuscan earth pigment, unroasted — sienna before the burn.

1608.name: Tequila Sunrise
1608.description: The layered dawn cocktail — grenadine sinking through orange juice, sunrise reconstructed in a glass.

1609.name: Bronze
1609.description: Third place's metal — copper and tin holding a podium spot.

1610.name: Latte
1610.description: Espresso softened with steamed milk — beige with barista art.

1611.name: Mango
1611.description: The tropical stone fruit at full orange — messy to eat, worth the shirt.

1612.name: Almond
1612.description: The nut in its skin-on tan.

1613.name: Apricot
1613.description: The velvet stone fruit — orange with a soft-focus filter.

1614.name: Peach
1614.description: The fruit that named the softest orange-pink.

1615.name: Peach Puff
1615.description: The X11 peach at powder-puff softness.

1616.name: Smell of Bakery
1616.description: Butter, yeast and sugar, radiating from the oven door — beige you can smell.

1617.name: Dwindling Dandelion
1617.description: The seed head at half strength — one puff already spent.

1618.name: Chocolate
1618.description: The bean, fermented, roasted and civilised into brown.

1619.name: Sepia
1619.description: The cuttlefish-ink brown of old photographs — nostalgia's official filter.

1620.name: Dark Goldenrod
1620.description: Goldenrod at dusk, gold banking to brown.

1621.name: Fawn
1621.description: The deer's first-year coat — tan that flatters everything.

1623.name: Monarch
1623.description: The migratory butterfly in orange-and-black — four generations to make one round trip.

1624.name: Peanut Butter Chicken
1624.description: The dish that shouldn't work and this colour that shouldn't either — both delicious.

1625.name: Honey Glow
1625.description: Honey with the lamp behind it.

1626.name: Combat Boot
1626.description: Parade-ground leather — brown-black with a mirror toe.

1627.name: Dried Grass
1627.description: The lawn in August, gone to straw.

1629.name: Golden Syrup
1629.description: The tin of amber the spoon stands up in — Britain's breakfast adhesive.

1631.name: School Bus Yellow
1631.description: The yellow legislated for visibility — school transport's official cheer.

1632.name: Amber
1632.description: Fossilised tree resin — prehistoric gold with the occasional mosquito tenant.

1633.name: Sunshine
1633.description: The forecast everyone wanted — yellow with a UV index.

1635.name: Swamp
1635.description: Standing water and secrets — olive-brown with methane.

1638.name: Brimstone
1638.description: The sulphurous yellow of fire-and-... sermons — brimstone is just old English for sulphur.

1639.name: Vetiver
1639.description: The bundled root grass of perfume's base notes — khaki-green scent anchor.

1640.name: Brass
1640.description: The section that plays loudest — alloy gold with valves.

1641.name: Manila Envelope
1641.description: The office envelope in its unbleached tan — named for Manila hemp.

1642.name: Bone
1642.description: The ivory-adjacent white of a well-boiled soup joint.

1643.name: Banana Drama
1643.description: A banana peel's third act — comedy in yellow.

1644.name: Banana
1644.description: The curved yellow standard of fruit bowls and slapstick.

1645.name: Sunbeam
1645.description: One shaft of yellow through a gap in the curtains.

1646.name: Cream
1646.description: The top of the milk — dairy's palest yellow.

1647.name: Cypress
1647.description: The tall dark conifer of Mediterranean cemeteries and Van Gogh skies.

1648.name: Ghillie Suit
1648.description: The sniper's shrubbery — a suit of olive rags pretending to be landscape.

1649.name: Bog Myrtle
1649.description: The bog shrub that scents Highland air and repels Highland midges — olive-gold.

1684.name: Forest
1684.description: A forest is an ecosystem characterized by a dense community of trees. Hundreds of definitions of forest are used throughout the world, incorporating factors such as tree density, tree height, land use, legal standing, and ecological function.

1689.name: Leaf
1689.description: A leaf is a principal appendage of the stem of a vascular plant, usually borne laterally above ground and specialized for photosynthesis. Leaves are collectively called foliage, as in "autumn foliage", while the leaves, stem, flower, and fruit collectively form the shoot system.

1696.name: Lettuce
1696.description: An annual plant of the family Asteraceae mostly grown as a leaf vegetable. The leaves are most often used raw in green salads, although lettuce is also seen in other kinds of food, such as sandwiches, wraps and soups; it can also be grilled.

1736.name: Alexandrite
1736.description: The mineral or gemstone chrysoberyl is an aluminate of beryllium with the formula Be Al2O4. The name chrysoberyl is derived from the Greek words χρυσός chrysos and βήρυλλος beryllos, meaning "a gold-white spar".

1786.name: Hummingbird
1786.description: Hummingbirds are birds native to the Americas and comprise the biological family Trochilidae. With approximately 375 species and 113 genera, they occur from Alaska to Tierra del Fuego, but most species are found in Central and South America.

1822.name: Freon
1822.description: A registered trademark of Chemours and generic descriptor for a number of halocarbon products. They are stable, nonflammable, low toxicity gases or liquids which have generally been used as refrigerants and as aerosol propellants.

1826.name: Fjord
1826.description: In physical geography, a fjord is a long, relatively narrow sea inlet with steep sides or cliffs in a valley created by a former glacier, which has since become inundated with water. They are the glacial equivalent of drowned river valleys, known as rias.

1852.name: Giotto
1852.description: Giotto di Bondone, known mononymously as Giotto, was an Italian painter and architect from Florence during the Late Middle Ages. He worked during the Gothic and Proto-Renaissance period.

1865.name: Nautical
1865.description: Seamanship is the art, competence, and knowledge of operating a ship, boat or other craft on water. The Oxford Dictionary states that seamanship is "The skill, techniques, or practice of handling a ship or boat at sea."

1866.name: Grandidierite
1866.description: A rare mineral that was first discovered in 1902 in southern Madagascar. The mineral was named in honor of French explorer Alfred Grandidier (1836–1912) who studied the natural history of Madagascar.

1867.name: Jodhpur
1867.description: The second-largest city of the north-western Indian state of Rajasthan, after its capital Jaipur. As of 2025, the city has a population of 1.6 million. It serves as the administrative headquarters of the Jodhpur district and Jodhpur division.

1973.name: Indigo
1973.description: A term used for a number of hues in the region of blue. The word comes from the ancient dye of the same name.

1979.name: Clematis
1979.description: A genus of about 380 species within the buttercup family, Ranunculaceae. Their garden hybrids and cultivars have been popular among gardeners, beginning with Clematis 'Jackmanii', a garden staple since 1862. More cultivars are being produced constantly, mainly of Chinese and Japanese origin.

2016.name: Fluorite
2016.description: Fluorite (also called fluorspar) is the mineral form of calcium fluoride, CaF2. It belongs to the halide minerals. It crystallizes in isometric cubic habit, although octahedral and more complex isometric forms are not uncommon.

2019.name: Full Bleed
2019.description: The debut studio album by the American duo Caught on Tape, a collaborative project by guitarist Thurston Moore and drummer John Moloney. It released on February 10, 2015, by Northern Spy Records.

2021.name: Sequin
2021.description: A sequin is a small, typically shiny, generally disk-shaped ornament.

2103.name: Raven
2103.description: A raven is any of several large-bodied passerine bird species in the genus Corvus. These species do not form a single taxonomic group within the genus. There is no consistent distinction between crows and ravens; the two names are assigned to different species mainly by size.

2144.name: Rhodonite
2144.description: A manganese inosilicate, with the formula (Mn, Fe, Mg, Ca)SiO3, and member of the pyroxenoid group of minerals, crystallizing in the triclinic system. The term rhodonite was first introduced by Germar. from Ancient Greek ῥόδον (rhódon) 'rose'.

2156.name: Camellia
2156.description: A genus of flowering plants in the family Theaceae. They are found in tropical and subtropical areas in eastern and southern Asia, from the Himalayas east to Japan and Indonesia. There are more than 220 described species; almost all are found in southern China and Indochina.

2181.name: Red Clay
2181.description: An album recorded in 1970 by jazz trumpeter Freddie Hubbard. It was his first album on Creed Taylor's CTI label and marked a shift toward the soul-jazz fusion sounds that would dominate his recordings in the later part of the decade.

2183.name: Maraschino
2183.description: A liqueur obtained from the distillation of Marasca cherries. The small, slightly sour fruit of the Marasca cherry tree, which grows wild along parts of the Dalmatian coast in Croatia, lends the liqueur its unique aroma.

2185.name: Fire Hydrant
2185.description: A fire hydrant, fireplug, firecock (archaic), hydrant riser or Johnny Pump is a connection point by which firefighters can tap into a water supply. It is a component of active fire protection. Underground fire hydrants have been used in Europe and Asia since at least the 18th century.

2200.name: Snapdragon
2200.description: Antirrhinum is a genus of plants in the Plantaginaceae family, commonly known as dragon flowers or snapdragons because of the flowers' fancied resemblance to the face of a dragon that opens and closes its mouth when laterally squeezed. They are also sometimes called toadflax or dog flower.

2208.name: Melon
2208.description: A melon is any of various plants of the family Cucurbitaceae with sweet, edible, and fleshy fruit. It can also specifically refer to Cucumis melo, commonly known as the "true melon" or simply "melon". The term "melon" can apply to both the plant and its fruit.

2243.name: Tiger
2243.description: A large cat and a member of the genus Panthera native to Asia. It has a powerful, muscular body with a large head and paws, a long tail and orange fur with black, mostly vertical stripes.

2310.name: Turmeric
2310.description: A flowering plant in the ginger family Zingiberaceae. It is a perennial, rhizomatous, herbaceous plant native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia that requires temperatures between 20 and 30 °C and high annual rainfall to thrive.

2339.name: Chamomile
2339.description: Chamomile or camomile is the common name for several daisy-like plants of the family Asteraceae. Two of the species, Matricaria chamomilla and Chamaemelum nobile, are commonly used to make herbal infusions for beverages.

2454.name: Peridot
2454.description: A yellow-green transparent variety of olivine. Peridot is one of the few gemstones that occur in only one color.

2521.name: Noctiluca
2521.description: A genus of dinoflagellates in the family Noctilucaceae. Its only species is Noctiluca scintillans, a marine species that can exist in a green or red form, depending on the pigmentation in its vacuoles.

2522.name: Peppermint Patty
2522.description: A fictional character featured in Charles M. Schulz's comic strip Peanuts. Her full name, very rarely used in the strip, is Patricia Reichardt. She is often confused with Patty, a character introduced earlier in the Peanuts Comics.

2524.name: Icecap
2524.description: In glaciology, an ice cap is a mass of ice that covers less than 50,000 km2 (19,000 mi2) of land area. Larger ice masses covering more than 50,000 km2 (19,000 mi2) are termed ice sheets.

2525.name: Paraiba
2525.description: Paraíba is a state in Brazil. It is located in the Brazilian Northeast, and it is bordered by Rio Grande do Norte to the north, Ceará to the west, Pernambuco to the south and the Atlantic Ocean to the east.

2526.name: Comb Jelly
2526.description: Ctenophora or ctenophores, commonly known as comb jellies, is a basal phylum of marine invertebrate animals notable for their unique aquatic locomotion using groups of cilia. They inhabit marine habitats worldwide, with 186 recognised living species.

2564.name: Crevasse
2564.description: A crevasse is a deep crack that forms in a glacier or ice sheet. Crevasses form as a result of the movement and resulting stress associated with the shear stress generated when two semi-rigid pieces above a plastic substrate have different rates of movement.

2614.name: Marina
2614.description: A marina is a dock or basin with moorings and supplies for yachts and small boats. A marina differs from a port in that a marina does not handle large passenger ships or cargo from freighters.

2667.name: Anglerfish
2667.description: Ray-finned fish in the order Lophiiformes. Both the order's common and scientific name comes from the characteristic mode of predation, in which a modified dorsal fin ray acts as a lure for prey.

2677.name: Grand Bleu
2677.description: The Frente Tricolor is the official supporters group of Suwon Samsung Bluewings.

2678.name: Kanagawa
2678.description: Kanagawa Prefecture is a prefecture of Japan located in the Kantō region of Honshu. Kanagawa Prefecture is the second-most populous prefecture of Japan at 9,221,129 and third-densest at 3,800 inhabitants per square kilometre (9,800/sq mi). Its geographic area of 2,415 km2 (932 sq mi) makes it fifth-smallest.

2679.name: Lobelia
2679.description: A genus of flowering plants in the family Campanulaceae comprising 444 species, with a subcosmopolitan distribution primarily in tropical to warm temperate regions of the world, a few species extending into cooler temperate regions. They are known generally as lobelias.

2708.name: Chefchaouen
2708.description: A city in northwest Morocco. It is the chief town of the province of the same name and is noted for its buildings in shades of blue, for which it is nicknamed the "Blue City". It is situated in a mountainous region in northern Morocco, between Tétouan and Ouazzane.

2762.name: Blue Öyster Cult
2762.description: An American rock band formed on Long Island, New York, in the hamlet of Stony Brook, in 1967. They have sold 25 million records worldwide, including 7 million in the United States.

2806.name: Tarot
2806.description: A set of playing cards used in Tarot card games and fortune-telling or divination. From at least the mid-15th century, the tarot was used to play trick-taking card games such as Tarocchini.

2811.name: Verbena
2811.description: A genus in the family Verbenaceae. It contains about 150 species of annual and perennial herbaceous or semi-woody flowering plants.

2856.name: Beetroot
2856.description: Beetroot or beet is the taproot portion of a Beta vulgaris subsp. vulgaris plant in the Conditiva Group. The plant is a root vegetable also known as the table beet, garden beet, or dinner beet, or else categorized by color as red beet or golden beet. Its leaves constitute a leaf vegetable called beet greens.

2862.name: Paisley Park
2862.description: A 65,000-square-foot (6,000 m2) estate in Chanhassen, Minnesota, United States, once owned by American musician Prince. It was opened to the public as a museum in October 2016. Many of Prince’s later albums would be recorded at the estate.

2864.name: Rhododendron
2864.description: Rhododendron, from Ancient Greek ῥόδον (rhódon), meaning "rose", and δένδρον (déndron), meaning "tree", is a very large genus of about 1,024 species of woody plants in the heath family (Ericaceae). They can be either evergreen or deciduous.

2865.name: Portulaca
2865.description: A genus of flowering plants in the family Portulacaceae, and is the type genus of the family. With over 100 species, it is found in the tropics and warm temperate regions. Portulacas are also known as the purslanes.

2884.name: Kunzite
2884.description: Spodumene is a pyroxene mineral consisting of lithium aluminium inosilicate, LiAl(SiO3)2, and is a commercially important source of lithium.

2930.name: Cabaret
2930.description: A form of theatrical entertainment featuring music, song, dance, recitation, or drama. The performance venue might be a pub, casino, hotel, restaurant, or nightclub with a stage for performances. The audience, often dining or drinking, does not typically dance but usually sits at tables.

2979.name: Festival
2979.description: A festival is an event celebrated by a community and centred on one or more characteristic aspects of its religion, traditions or cultures. It is often observed as an indigenous or national holiday, a religious celebration, or a cultural fair (mela).

2982.name: Carnival
2982.description: A festive season at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras.

3003.name: Umbra
3003.description: The umbra, penumbra and antumbra are three distinct parts of a shadow, created by any light source after impinging on an opaque object of lesser size. In cases of equal or larger impinging objects, only an umbra and penumbra are generated. Assuming no diffraction, for a collimated beam of light, only the umbra is cast.

3005.name: Lead
3005.description: A chemical element with the symbol Pb and atomic number 82. It is a heavy metal, denser than most common materials. Lead is soft, malleable, and has a relatively low melting point. When freshly cut or melted, it appears shiny silvery with a bluish tint, but tarnishes to dull gray on exposure to air.

3008.name: Oil
3008.description: A liquid with varying degrees of viscosity depending on temperature. Oil is any nonpolar chemical substance that is composed primarily of hydrocarbons and is hydrophobic and lipophilic. Oils are usually flammable and surface active. Most oils are unsaturated lipids that are liquid at room temperature.

3010.name: Cowboy
3010.description: A cowboy is an animal herder who tends cattle on ranches in North America, traditionally on horseback, and often performs a multitude of other ranch-related tasks.

3011.name: Tuna
3011.description: A saltwater fish that belongs to the tribe Thunnini, a subgrouping of the Scombridae (mackerel) family. The Thunnini comprise 15 species across five genera, the sizes of which vary from the bullet tuna to the Atlantic bluefin tuna, which averages 2 m (6.6 ft) and is believed to live up to 50 years.

3013.name: Liver
3013.description: A major metabolic organ exclusively found in vertebrates, which performs many essential biological functions such as detoxification of the organism, and the synthesis of various proteins and various other biochemicals necessary for digestion and growth.

3014.name: Lynx
3014.description: A lynx is any of the four extant species within the medium-sized wild cat genus Lynx. The name originated in Middle English via Latin from the Greek word lynx, derived from the Indo-European root *leuk-, in reference to the luminescence of its reflective eyes.

3015.name: Iron
3015.description: A chemical element; it has symbol Fe and atomic number 26. It is a metal that belongs to the first transition series and group 8 of the periodic table. It is, by mass, the most common element on Earth, forming much of Earth's outer and inner core. It is the fourth most abundant element in the Earth's crust.

3017.name: Morel
3017.description: Morchella, the true morels, is a genus of edible sac fungi closely related to anatomically simpler cup fungi in the order Pezizales. These distinctive fungi have a honeycomb appearance due to the network of ridges with pits composing their caps.

3019.name: Secret Passage
3019.description: A 2004 film directed by Ademir Kenović. It was written by Kenović and Olivier Bonas. The film stars John Turturro, Katherine Borowitz, Tara Fitzgerald, and Hannah Taylor-Gordon. In the United Kingdom, the film is mostly known as The Lion's Mouth.

3020.name: Rum
3020.description: A liquor made by fermenting and then distilling sugarcane molasses or sugarcane juice. The distillate, initially a clear liquid, is often aged in barrels.

3022.name: Gull
3022.description: Gulls, informally called seagulls, are seabirds of the subfamily Larinae. They are most closely related to terns and skimmers, and are placed with them in the family Laridae. They are also related, more distantly, to auks and skuas, and more distantly still to waders.

3023.name: Tin
3023.description: A chemical element; it has the symbol Sn and atomic number 50. A metallic-gray metal, tin is soft enough to be cut with little force, and a bar of tin can be bent by hand with little effort. When bent, a bar of tin makes a sound, the so-called "tin cry", as a result of twinning in tin crystals.

3030.name: Milk
3030.description: Usually a white liquid food produced by the mammary glands of lactating mammals. It is the primary source of nutrition for young mammals before they are able to digest solid food.

3036.name: Jazz
3036.description: A music genre that originated in the African-American communities of New Orleans, Louisiana, in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Its roots are in blues, ragtime, European harmony, African rhythmic rituals, spirituals, hymns, marches, vaudeville song, and dance music.

3052.name: Pachinko
3052.description: A mechanical game originating in Japan that is used as an arcade game and, much more frequently, for gambling. Pachinko fills a niche in Japanese gambling comparable to that of the slot machine in the Western world as a form of low-stakes, low-strategy gambling.

3085.name: Lonestar
3085.description: An American country music band from Nashville, Tennessee. The band consists of Drew Womack, Michael Britt, Dean Sams, and Keech Rainwater (drums). Both Britt and Rainwater formerly recorded in the band Canyon.

3097.name: Monza
3097.description: A city and comune (municipality) in the Lombardy region of northern Italy. Situated on the Lambro river about 15 kilometres (9.3 mi) north-northeast of Milan, it is the capital of the Province of Monza and Brianza and the largest city in the Brianza area.

3100.name: Signal
3100.description: A signal is both the process and the result of transmission of data over some media accomplished by embedding some variation. Signals are important in multiple subject fields, including signal processing, information theory and biology.

3102.name: Mario
3102.description: A character created by Japanese video game designer Shigeru Miyamoto. He is the star of the Mario franchise, a recurring character in the Donkey Kong franchise, and the mascot of their owner, the Japanese company Nintendo.

3117.name: Dynamite
3117.description: An explosive made of nitroglycerin, sorbents, and stabilizers. It was invented by the Swedish chemist and engineer Alfred Nobel in Geesthacht, Northern Germany, and was patented in 1867. It rapidly gained wide-scale use as a more robust alternative to the traditional black powder explosives.

3123.name: Meat
3123.description: Animal tissue, mostly muscle, that is eaten as food. Humans have hunted and farmed other animals for meat since prehistory. The Neolithic Revolution allowed the domestication of vertebrates, including chickens, sheep, goats, pigs, horses, and cattle, starting around 11,000 years ago.

3153.name: Magma
3153.description: The molten or semi-molten natural material from which all igneous rocks are formed. Magma is found beneath the surface of the Earth, and evidence of magmatism has also been discovered on other terrestrial planets and some natural satellites.

3166.name: Taco
3166.description: A taco is a traditional Mexican dish consisting of a small hand-sized corn- or wheat-based tortilla topped with a filling. The tortilla is then folded around the filling and eaten by hand.

3170.name: Bison
3170.description: A bison is a large bovine in the genus Bison within the tribe Bovini. Two extant and numerous extinct species are recognised.

3178.name: Jaffa
3178.description: An ancient Levantine port city which is part of Tel Aviv-Yafo, Israel, located in its southern part. The city sits atop a naturally elevated outcrop on the Mediterranean coastline.

3180.name: Cheese
3180.description: A type of dairy product produced in a range of flavors, textures, and forms by coagulation of the milk protein casein. It is composed of proteins and fat from milk, usually of cows, goats or sheep, and sometimes of water buffalo.

3184.name: Bigfoot
3184.description: A large, hairy, mythical humanoid creature said to inhabit forests in North America, particularly in the Pacific Northwest.

3186.name: Eagle
3186.description: The common name for certain large birds of prey within the family of the Accipitridae. While on a genetic level, only the subfamily Aquilinae comprises "true eagles", many other species are commonly referred to as eagles, such as the bald eagle, and the term generally carries no taxonomic weight.

3188.name: Hemp
3188.description: A plant in the botanical class of Cannabis sativa cultivars grown specifically for industrial and consumable use. It can be used to make a wide range of products. Along with bamboo, hemp is among the fastest growing plants on Earth.

3189.name: Peru
3189.description: A country in western South America. It is bordered to the north by Ecuador and Colombia, to the east by Brazil, to the southeast by Bolivia, to the south by Chile, and to the south and west by the Pacific Ocean.

3191.name: Pizza
3191.description: An Italian dish typically consisting of a flat base of leavened wheat-based dough topped with tomato, cheese, and other ingredients, baked at a high temperature, traditionally in a wood-fired oven.

3192.name: Cognac
3192.description: A variety of brandy named after the commune of Cognac, France. It is produced in the surrounding departments of Charente and Charente-Maritime, in an officially designated wine-growing region. This region is divided into six districts with different cognacs produced in each.

3197.name: Brandy
3197.description: A liquor produced by distilling wine. Brandy generally contains 35–60% alcohol by volume and is typically consumed as an after-dinner digestif. Some brandies are aged in wooden casks.

3198.name: Pasta
3198.description: A type of food typically made from an unleavened dough of wheat flour mixed with water or eggs, and formed into sheets or other shapes, then cooked by boiling or baking.

3201.name: Cola
3201.description: A carbonated soft drink flavored with vanilla, cinnamon, citrus oils, and other flavorings. The name, however, comes from the kola nut, which imbues the beverage with caffeine. A stimulating soda using kola appeared circa 1880.

3234.name: Star
3234.description: A star is a luminous spheroid of plasma held together by self-gravity. The nearest star to Earth is the Sun. Many other stars are visible to the naked eye at night; their immense distances from Earth make them appear as fixed points of light.

3277.name: Pea
3277.description: A pulse or fodder crop, but the word often refers to the seed or sometimes the pod of this flowering plant species. Peas are eaten as a vegetable.

3330.name: Metal
3330.description: A metal is a material that, when polished or fractured, shows a lustrous appearance, and conducts electricity and heat relatively well. These properties are all associated with having electrons available at the Fermi level, as opposed to nonmetallic materials which do not. Metals are typically ductile and malleable.

3348.name: Acid
3348.description: An acid is a molecule or ion capable of either donating a proton (i.e. hydrogen cation, H+), known as a Brønsted–Lowry acid, or forming a covalent bond with an electron pair, known as a Lewis acid.

3353.name: Radioactive
3353.description: Radioactive decay is the process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by radiation. A material containing unstable nuclei is considered radioactive. Three of the most common types of decay are alpha, beta, and gamma decay.

3355.name: Futon
3355.description: A futon is a traditional Japanese style of bedding.

3357.name: Liquorice
3357.description: Liquorice or licorice is the common name of Glycyrrhiza glabra, a flowering plant of the bean family Fabaceae, from the root of which a sweet, aromatic flavouring is extracted.

3363.name: Cape Verde
3363.description: Cape Verde, also referred to in English by its Portuguese name Cabo Verde, and known officially as the Republic of Cabo Verde, is an archipelagic country in the central Atlantic Ocean, off the coast of West Africa.

3369.name: Jurassic Park
3369.description: A 1993 American science fiction adventure film directed by Steven Spielberg and written by Michael Crichton and David Koepp, based on Crichton's 1990 novel.

3386.name: Money
3386.description: Any item or verifiable record that is generally accepted as payment for goods and services and repayment of debts, such as taxes, in a particular country or socio-economic context.

3407.name: Moth
3407.description: Moths are a group of winged insect of the order Lepidoptera, the other well-known members of which are butterflies, they are characterized by dark or dull patterned wings.

3422.name: Caribbean
3422.description: A geo-political region in the middle of the Americas centered around the Caribbean Sea in the North Atlantic Ocean, mostly overlapping with the West Indies.

3444.name: Pigeon
3444.description: Columbidae is a bird family consisting of doves and pigeons. It is the only family in the order Columbiformes. These are stout-bodied birds with small heads, relatively short necks and slender bills that in some species feature fleshy ceres.

3458.name: Nature
3458.description: The physical world or universe. In this general sense nature refers to the laws, elements and phenomena of the physical world, including life.

3476.name: Ice
3476.description: Water that is frozen into a solid state, typically forming at or below temperatures of 0 °C, 32 °F, or 273.15 K. It occurs naturally on Earth, on other planets, in Oort cloud objects, and as interstellar ice.

3510.name: Opal
3510.description: A hydrated amorphous form of silica (SiO2·nH2O); its water content may range from 3% to 21% by weight, but is usually between 6% and 10%. Due to the amorphous (chemical) physical structure, it is classified as a mineraloid, unlike crystalline forms of silica, which are considered minerals.

3517.name: Frostbite
3517.description: An injury to skin or other living tissue that is allowed to freeze, especially affecting the fingers, toes, nose, ears, cheeks and chin.

3519.name: Carbon
3519.description: A chemical element; it has symbol C and atomic number 6. It is nonmetallic and tetravalent—meaning that its atoms are able to form up to four covalent bonds due to its valence shell exhibiting 4 electrons. It belongs to group 14 of the periodic table. Carbon makes up about 0.025 percent of Earth's crust.

3521.name: Lacrosse
3521.description: A contact team sport played with a lacrosse stick and a lacrosse ball. It is the oldest organized sport in North America, with its origins with the indigenous people of North America as early as the 12th century.

3523.name: Shipyard
3523.description: A shipyard, also called a dockyard or boatyard, is a place where ships are built and repaired. These can be yachts, military vessels, cruise liners or other cargo or passenger ships.

3524.name: Ming
3524.description: The Ming dynasty, officially the Great Ming, was an imperial dynasty of China that ruled from 1368 to 1644, following the collapse of the Mongol-led Yuan dynasty. The Ming was the last imperial dynasty of China ruled by the Han people, the majority ethnic group in China.

3526.name: Panorama
3526.description: A panorama is any wide-angle view or representation of a physical space, whether in painting, drawing, photography, film, seismic images, or 3D modeling. The word was coined in the 18th century by the English painter Robert Barker to describe his panoramic paintings of Edinburgh and London.

3527.name: Catfish
3527.description: A diverse group of ray-finned fish of the order Siluriformes. Catfish are named for their prominent barbels, which resemble a cat's whiskers, though not all catfish have prominent barbels. All Siluriformes lack scales, instead possessing either smooth skin or armour-plated bodies.

3553.name: Leviathan
3553.description: A giant sea serpent noted in theology and mythology. It is referenced in the Hebrew Bible, as a metaphor for a powerful enemy, notably Babylon. It is referred to in Psalms, the Book of Job, the Book of Isaiah, and the Book of Enoch.

3584.name: Nor'easter
3584.description: A nor'easter is a large-scale extratropical cyclone in the western North Atlantic Ocean. The name derives from the direction of the winds that blow from the northeast. Typically, such storms originate as a low-pressure area that forms within 100 miles (160 km) of the shore between North Carolina and Massachusetts.

3618.name: Dragonfly
3618.description: A dragonfly is a flying insect belonging to the infraorder Anisoptera below the order Odonata. About 3,000 extant species of dragonflies are known. Most are tropical, with fewer species in temperate regions. Loss of wetland habitat threatens dragonfly populations around the world.

3635.name: Citadel
3635.description: A citadel is the most fortified area of a town or city. It may be a castle, fortress, or fortified center. The term is a diminutive of city, meaning "little city", because it is a smaller part of the city of which it is the defensive core.

3647.name: Nickel
3647.description: A chemical element; it has symbol Ni and atomic number 28. It is a silvery-white lustrous metal with a slight golden tinge. Nickel is a hard and ductile transition metal.

3689.name: Sailing
3689.description: Sailing employs the wind—acting on sails, wing sails or kites—to propel a craft on the surface of the water, on ice (iceboat) or on land over a chosen course, which is often part of a larger plan of navigation.

3693.name: Windjammer
3693.description: A windjammer is a commercial sailing ship with multiple masts, however rigged. The informal term "windjammer" arose during the transition from the Age of Sail to the Age of Steam during the 19th century.

3694.name: Raincoat
3694.description: A raincoat is a waterproof or water-resistant garment worn on the upper body to shield the wearer from rain. The term rain jacket is sometimes used to refer to raincoats with long sleeves that are waist-length. A rain jacket may be combined with a pair of rain pants to make a rainsuit.

3720.name: Scampi
3720.description: A crustacean-based seafood dish, typically featuring langoustine, shrimp or prawns sautéed in olive oil, garlic, and white wine, and garnished with Parmesan cheese and lemon juice, though exact ingredients vary regionally in preparation.

3729.name: Old World
3729.description: "Old World" is a term for Afro-Eurasia coined by Europeans after 1493, when they became aware of the existence of the Americas, Oceania, and Antarctica.

3744.name: Queens of the Dead
3744.description: A 2025 American zombie comedy film written and directed by Tina Romero. It stars Katy O'Brian, Jaquel Spivey, Riki Lindhome, Jack Haven, Cheyenne Jackson, and Margaret Cho.

3746.name: Twilight
3746.description: Daylight illumination produced by diffuse sky radiation when the Sun is below the horizon as sunlight from the upper atmosphere is scattered in a way that illuminates both the Earth's lower atmosphere and also the Earth's surface.

3766.name: Flood
3766.description: A flood is an overflow of water that submerges land that is usually dry. In the sense of "flowing water", the word may also be applied to the inflow of the tide. Floods are of significant concern in agriculture, civil engineering and public health.

3778.name: Crow
3778.description: A crow is a bird of the genus Corvus, or more broadly, a synonym for all of Corvus. The word "crow" is used as part of the common name of many species. The related term "raven" is not linked scientifically to any certain trait but is rather a general grouping for larger-sized species of Corvus.

3781.name: Voldemort
3781.description: Lord Voldemort is a fictional character and the main antagonist in the Harry Potter series of novels by J. K. Rowling.

3834.name: Purple Heart
3834.description: The Purple Heart (PH) is a United States military decoration awarded in the name of the president to those wounded or killed while serving on or after 5 April 1917, with the U.S. military.

3841.name: Opera
3841.description: A form of Western theatre in which music is a fundamental component and dramatic roles are taken by singers. Such a "work" is typically a collaboration between a composer and a librettist and incorporates a number of the performing arts, such as acting, scenery, costume, and sometimes dance or ballet.

3842.name: Nebula
3842.description: A nebula is a distinct luminescent part of interstellar medium, which can consist of ionized, neutral, or molecular hydrogen and also cosmic dust. Nebulae are often star-forming regions, such as the Pillars of Creation in the Eagle Nebula.

3848.name: Macarena
3848.description: "Macarena" is a song by Spanish pop duo Los del Río, originally recorded for their 1993 album A mí me gusta. A dance remix by the electropop group Fangoria was a success in Spain, and a soundalike cover version by Los del Mar became popular in Canada.

3863.name: Pinkman
3863.description: Jesse Bruce Pinkman is a fictional character in the American crime drama television series Breaking Bad, portrayed by Aaron Paul. He is a crystal meth cook and dealer who works with his former high school chemistry teacher, Walter White.

3865.name: Pride Parade
3865.description: A pride parade is an event where lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and queer (LGBTQ) people and their allies celebrate LGBTQ social and self-acceptance, achievements, legal rights, and pride. The events sometimes also serve as demonstrations for legal rights such as same-sex marriage.

3876.name: Lychee
3876.description: Lychees are the fruit of the tree Litchi chinensis, the sole member of the genus Litchi, part of the soapberry family, Sapindaceae. They are edible, with a sweet, mildly tart flavor, and have a distinctive floral aroma often described as rose-like.

3951.name: Tayberry
3951.description: A cultivated shrub in the genus Rubus of the family Rosaceae patented in 1979 as a cross between a blackberry and a red raspberry, and named after the River Tay in Scotland.

4010.name: Sesame
4010.description: A plant in the genus Sesamum, also called benne. Numerous wild relatives occur in Africa and a smaller number in India. It is widely naturalized in tropical regions around the world and is cultivated for its edible seeds, which grow in pods.

4013.name: Pearl
4013.description: A pearl is a hard, glistening object produced within the soft tissue of a living shelled mollusk or another animal, such as fossil conulariids. Just like the shell of a mollusk, a pearl is composed of calcium carbonate in minute crystalline form, which is deposited in concentric layers.

4015.name: Linen
4015.description: A textile made from the bast fibers of the flax plant. Linen cloth has been produced since ancient times primarily for use in clothing and household items.

4028.name: Tandoori
4028.description: A tandoor is a large vase-shaped oven, usually made of clay. Since antiquity, tandoors have been used to bake unleavened flatbreads, such as roti and traditional lavash, as well as leavened ones, such as naan and tandoor bread or matnakash. It is also used to roast meat and vegetables.

4030.name: Carpaccio
4030.description: A dish of meat or fish, thinly sliced or pounded thin, and served raw, typically as an appetiser. It was invented in 1950 by Giuseppe Cipriani, founder of Harry's Bar in Venice, Italy, and popularised during the second half of the twentieth century.

4032.name: Fuzzy Wuzzy
4032.description: Since the introduction of Crayola drawing crayons by Binney & Smith in 1903, more than 200 colors have been produced in a wide variety of assortments. The table below represents all of the colors found in regular Crayola assortments from 1903 to the present.

4042.name: Granite
4042.description: A coarse-grained (phaneritic) intrusive igneous rock composed mostly of quartz, alkali feldspar, mica and plagioclase. It forms from magma with a high content of silica and alkali metal oxides that slowly cools and solidifies underground.

4195.name: Faience
4195.description: Faience or faïence is the general English language term for fine tin-glazed pottery. The invention of a white pottery glaze suitable for painted decoration, by the addition of an oxide of tin to the slip of a lead glaze, was a major advance in the history of pottery.

4197.name: Guacamole
4197.description: An avocado-based dip, spread, or salad first developed in Mexico. In addition to its use in modern Mexican cuisine, it has become part of international cuisine as a dip, condiment, and salad ingredient.

4292.name: International Klein Blue
4292.description: International Klein Blue (IKB) is a deep blue hue first mixed by the French artist Yves Klein. IKB's visual impact comes from its heavy reliance on ultramarine, as well as Klein's often thick and textured application of paint to canvas.

4383.name: Shiitake
4383.description: A macrofungus native to East Asia and mainland Southeast Asia, which is cultivated and consumed around the globe.

4404.name: Chinese New Year
4404.description: Chinese New Year, also known as the Spring Festival, marks the beginning of a new year on the traditional lunisolar Chinese calendar. It is one of the most important holidays in Chinese culture.

0000.name: Black
0000.description: The complete absorption of visible light — though this one, like every black a screen can show, keeps a whisper of blue in it.

0001.name: Melanite
0001.description: A lustrous black variety of andradite garnet, named from the Greek for black; Victorian jewellers cut it for mourning wear.

0002.name: Soot
0002.description: Unburnt carbon settling out of a flame — the first black pigment humans ever put on a cave wall.

0003.name: Cosmic Bit Flip
0003.description: When a stray cosmic ray strikes a memory cell and flips a 0 to a 1 — near-black with a single green bit set.

0004.name: Ink
0004.description: Iron-gall dark, the colour of every signature before ballpoints; never quite pure black once it dries.

0005.name: Black Olive
0005.description: Brine-cured and glistening on the antipasti plate — a black that leans quietly toward its green past.

0006.name: Obsidian
0006.description: Volcanic glass, cooled too fast to crystallise; knapped into blades sharper than surgical steel.

0007.name: Bats Cloak
0007.description: The soft dark of folded wings in a cave roof — black with a purple flicker when it moves.

0008.name: Dark Forest
0008.description: Past the treeline at dusk, where green stops being a colour and becomes a depth.

0009.name: Espresso
0009.description: A shot pulled short — crema-brown crossing over into black.

0010.name: Dark Slate
0010.description: Roof slate wet with rain, split along the grain.

0011.name: Dark Taupe
0011.description: Taupe is French for mole; this is the mole at the bottom of the burrow.

0012.name: Starling
0012.description: Not truly black at all — starling feathers are iridescent, flashing blue-violet when the murmuration turns.

0013.name: Walnut
0013.description: Oiled walnut heartwood; also the husk stain that won't leave your hands for a week.

0014.name: Witches Cauldron
0014.description: Cast iron in low candlelight, with something faintly purple simmering in it.

0015.name: Smoky Purple
0015.description: Purple seen through the last of the bonfire.

0016.name: Charcoal
0016.description: Wood baked without air until only carbon remains — the sketcher's black that is really a deep grey-teal.

0017.name: Cocoa
0017.description: Ground cacao before the sugar arrives: bitter, dusty, brown.

0018.name: Driftwood
0018.description: Timber the sea has sanded and the salt has greyed.

0019.name: Dusty Mauve
0019.description: Mauve after decades in the attic — the first aniline-dye colour, faded to a murmur.

0020.name: Artichoke
0020.description: The grey-green bract of a vegetable that is technically a thistle you eat with butter.

0021.name: Heather
0021.description: Moorland heather between blooms — grey until the light warms it purple.

0022.name: Stone
0022.description: Field-wall stone: no colour in particular, which is exactly its colour.

0023.name: Slate
0023.description: Split roofing slate, blue-grey along the cleavage plane.

0024.name: Sage
0024.description: Dried sage leaves, grey-green and dusty with their own oils.

0025.name: Harbour
0025.description: Harbour water on an overcast morning — grey-green, patient, smelling of rope and diesel.

0026.name: Lavender Grey
0026.description: Lavender with the scent faded out of it; grey with a floral memory.

0027.name: Laurel
0027.description: The wreath leaf of poets and victors, silvered green.

0028.name: Grey
0028.description: The exact midpoint of indifference — neither warm nor cool, and content with it.

0029.name: Rosy Grey
0029.description: Grey that has been standing near a blush.

0030.name: Putty
0030.description: Glazier's putty: linseed and chalk, the quiet beige holding every old window in place.

0031.name: Warm Grey
0031.description: Grey with the thermostat nudged up a degree.

0032.name: Silver
0032.description: Polished-metal grey — the matte version, before the mirror finish.

0033.name: Greige
0033.description: The portmanteau interior designers reach for when beige and grey refuse to commit.

0034.name: Wild Life
0034.description: Pale grey-cyan of dawn mist where something just moved in the reeds.

0035.name: Mecha Kitty
0035.description: Pastel armour plating for a robot cat — lilac-grey chrome from a Saturday-morning anime.

0036.name: Windy
0036.description: The sky on a day that can't sit still — pale grey-cyan with weather in it.

0037.name: Beige
0037.description: From the French for undyed wool: fabric before fashion happens to it.

0038.name: Sugared Almond
0038.description: Dragée-coated almonds in pale pink — the confetti candy handed out at weddings.

0039.name: Morning Mist
0039.description: The hour when the garden is only shapes.

0040.name: Velvet Scarf
0040.description: Pale violet velvet, worn once, kept forever.

0041.name: Ivory
0041.description: Tusk-white with age in it; the reason old piano keys yellowed so gracefully.

0042.name: Perfume Haze
0042.description: The pale violet cloud left hanging after someone elegant leaves the room.

0043.name: Frost
0043.description: First frost on a window — white with the faintest blue instruction to stay inside.

0044.name: White
0044.description: All wavelengths at once — here with the warm cast of paper rather than the cold of a screen.

0045.name: Black Cherry
0045.description: Cherries cooked down to their darkest; the sundae flavour and the near-black red.

0046.name: Kermes
0046.description: The ancient scarlet dye crushed from scale insects on Mediterranean oaks — crimson's oldest ancestor.

0047.name: Merlot
0047.description: The softest of the big red grapes, poured in low light.

0048.name: Cochineal
0048.description: Carmine's source: cactus-dwelling insects, dried and crushed — still colouring lipsticks and aperitifs.

0049.name: Boysenberry
0049.description: The bramble cross of raspberry, blackberry and loganberry, named for Rudolph Boysen; dark-juice territory.

0050.name: Wine
0050.description: Red wine at table depth — the glass half-empty of light.

0051.name: Mulberry
0051.description: The berry that stains everything it touches, from the silkworm's favourite tree.

0052.name: Garance
0052.description: French madder — the dye that reddened a million army trousers before synthetic alizarin arrived.

0053.name: Fig
0053.description: The skin of a ripe fig, purple-brown under a dusty bloom.

0054.name: Alizarin
0054.description: The red of madder root and the first natural dye ever synthesised in a lab (1868); beloved of watercolourists since.

0055.name: Carmine
0055.description: Cochineal's concentrated crimson, once traded at more than its weight in silver.

0056.name: Cranberry
0056.description: Bog-grown and sharp — the sauce, not the juice.

0057.name: Rubine
0057.description: Printer's process red from the rubine pigment family — magenta's punchier cousin.

0058.name: Dark Raspberry
0058.description: Raspberries at the bottom of the punnet, crushed a little.

0060.name: Cerise
0060.description: French for cherry, English for a pink that means business.

0061.name: Rhodamine
0061.description: The fluorescent dye that makes safety pink glow under UV; beloved of microscopists and highlighter engineers.

0062.name: Ruby
0062.description: Corundum with chromium in it — the red that made crowns worth stealing.

0063.name: Rosewood
0063.description: The rose-scented hardwood of guitar fretboards.

0064.name: Crimson Lake
0064.description: A 'lake' is a dye fixed onto powder to make paint — this one is cochineal crimson, a watercolour-box staple.

0065.name: Rose Red
0065.description: The rose of valentines, one shade before cliché.

0066.name: Antique Ruby
0066.description: A ruby that has been in the family long enough to have stories.

0067.name: Yearning Desire
0067.description: Magenta-red pitched exactly at the temperature of wanting something you shouldn't.

0068.name: Fuchsine
0068.description: The 1858 aniline dye named after the fuchsia flower — magenta's original trade name.

0069.name: Punch
0069.description: The bowl at the party: red fruit, questionable additions.

0070.name: Incarnadine
0070.description: Shakespeare's verb — 'the multitudinous seas incarnadine' — for turning things blood-red.

0071.name: Cinnapink
0071.description: Cinnamon dust settling on pink icing.

0072.name: Rouge
0072.description: The compact in every 1940s handbag — cheek-red by way of Paris.

0073.name: Watermelon
0073.description: The flesh an inch in from the rind, where the sweetness starts.

0074.name: Old Rose
0074.description: A rose pressed in a book by someone's grandmother.

0075.name: Rosella
0075.description: The crimson-splashed Australian parrot — and the hibiscus cordial that borrows its name.

0076.name: Quail
0076.description: The soft rosy grey-brown of quail plumage.

0077.name: Roseine
0077.description: An early trade name for magenta dye, taken from the rose.

0078.name: Radicchio
0078.description: The bitter chicory whose leaves are wine-red veined with white.

0079.name: Opium
0079.description: A den's haze: mauve-grey and heavy-lidded.

0080.name: Strawberry
0080.description: Ripe at the tip, red all the way through if you're lucky.

0081.name: Punk Pink
0081.description: Pink with the safety pin still in it.

0082.name: Carnation
0082.description: The buttonhole flower; 'carnation' and 'incarnadine' share a root meaning flesh.

0083.name: Erythrine
0083.description: From the Greek erythros, red — an old mineralogist's word for cobalt bloom.

0084.name: Neapolitan
0084.description: The stripe where the strawberry third meets the rest of the block.

0085.name: Hot Pink
0085.description: Pink past the thermostat's limit — the shade that made shocking pink jealous.

0086.name: Wild Strawberry
0086.description: The tiny woodland kind — more perfume than fruit.

0087.name: Hot Lips
0087.description: The kiss print left on the mirror.

0088.name: Solferino
0088.description: Named for the 1859 battle whose carnage inspired the Red Cross — and, in the same year, a magenta dye.

0089.name: Hindu Lotus
0089.description: The sacred pink lotus of South Asia, rising unstained from muddy water — a symbol of purity across traditions.

0090.name: Flamingo
0090.description: Pink on one leg — the bird dyes itself with the carotenoids in its shrimp dinner.

0091.name: Begonia
0091.description: The waxy bedding flower that blooms in relentless pink.

0092.name: Eosin
0092.description: The pink half of every microscope slide's H&E stain, named for Eos, goddess of dawn.

0095.name: Ultra Pink
0095.description: Pink dialled past what ordinary screens were built for.

0096.name: Guava
0096.description: The blushing flesh inside an unassuming green rind.

0097.name: Hyperbloom
0097.description: A flower bed rendered at 120 frames per second.

0098.name: Peony
0098.description: The flower that opens too generously for its own stem.

0099.name: Highlighter Pink
0099.description: The pink marker — for passages you'll definitely read again.

0100.name: Wallflower
0100.description: The quiet dusty pink at the edge of the dance floor — also a real flower that thrives in old walls.

0101.name: Phlox
0101.description: From the Greek for flame — a wildflower that burns pink.

0102.name: Peaches of Immortality
0102.description: In Chinese myth, the peaches in Xi Wangmu's garden ripen once every few thousand years and grant eternal life — rosy fruit worth the wait.

0103.name: Strawberry Taffy
0103.description: Pulled candy, strawberry flavour — pink stretched until it goes pale at the folds.

0104.name: Roseate
0104.description: The poet's adjective for rose-tinted, as in spoonbills and outlooks.

0105.name: Dayglo Pink
0105.description: Fluorescent poster pink borrowed from the Day-Glo palette of 1960s gig flyers.

0106.name: Bubblegum
0106.description: The first bubblegum's colour — pink only because its inventor had a single dye on hand in 1928.

0107.name: Marabou
0107.description: The feather trim on a showgirl's robe, dyed pink.

0108.name: Rhodolite
0108.description: The raspberry-pink garnet, from the Greek for rose stone.

0109.name: Azalea
0109.description: The shrub that turns whole hillsides pink each spring; Japan and Korea hold festivals under it.

0110.name: Fruity Licious
0110.description: A pink that tastes like the artificial flavour simply called 'fruit'.

0111.name: Petal Pink
0111.description: The inside of a petal, near the stem, where the colour is shyest.

0112.name: Upstream
0112.description: The direction salmon insist on — pink against the current.

0113.name: Cotton Candy
0113.description: Spun sugar: mostly air, entirely childhood.

0114.name: Motel Sign
0114.description: Neon pink buzzing VACANCY at the edge of the highway.

0115.name: Pink Blush
0115.description: The blush you can't will away.

0116.name: Poodle Skirt
0116.description: 1950s felt in sock-hop pink, appliquéd dog included.

0117.name: Shell Pink
0117.description: The inside of a seashell held up to the light.

0118.name: Pink
0118.description: The canonical light red; English only settled on 'pink' as a colour word in the late 1600s, borrowing the flower's name.

0119.name: Gossamer Pink
0119.description: Spider silk at sunrise — pink you can see through.

0120.name: Dark Garnet
0120.description: Garnet at the bottom of the jewellery box: all depth, almost no light.

0121.name: Dark Mahogany
0121.description: The boardroom table after a century of polish.

0122.name: Sang de Boeuf
0122.description: 'Ox blood' — the perilous copper-red glaze Chinese potters perfected in the Kangxi era.

0123.name: Oxblood
0123.description: Deep red leather with brown intentions — the classic boot colourway.

0124.name: Cabernet
0124.description: The structured grape: tannin you can see.

0125.name: Dragon's Blood
0125.description: A real resin from Dracaena trees, sold as incense and varnish since antiquity — allegedly dragon, actually tree.

0126.name: Maroon
0126.description: From the French marron, chestnut — the dark red of school uniforms everywhere.

0127.name: Pomegranate
0127.description: The fruit you open like a geode; garnet seeds by the hundred.

0128.name: Brick Dust
0128.description: What's left on your hands after a day of laying reds.

0129.name: Redwood
0129.description: Sequoia heartwood — the red of two-thousand-year patience.

0130.name: Sanguine
0130.description: Blood-red in Latin, cheerful in temperament — the old theory of humours let it be both.

0132.name: Dark Crimson
0132.description: Crimson after the theatre lights go down.

0133.name: Cherry Bomb
0133.description: The firework and the Runaways song — both this red.

0134.name: Chestnut
0134.description: Conker-shell red-brown, fresh from the husk and briefly glossy.

0135.name: Cardinal
0135.description: The red of the songbird and the robe — the bird was named after the cleric, not the other way round.

0137.name: Hot Lava
0137.description: Molten rock where the crust splits — a red that means don't.

0138.name: Madder Lake
0138.description: Madder root fixed into a painter's lake pigment; the old masters glazed shadows with it.

0139.name: Auburn

0167.name: Dusty Coral

0168.name: Grapefruit

0179.name: Glowing Coral

0230.description: Melon-ball orange, scooped on a hot day.
